project_overview.project_name: Productive Safety Net for Socioeconomic Opportunities Project (SNSOP)
project_overview.project_development_objective: To provide cash transfers and access to income generating opportunities and strengthen the National Safety Net Delivery System
project_overview.beneficiaries: 96,000 poor and vulnerable households (approximately 672,000 individuals), including 7,000 refugee households and 10,500 host community households

primary_locations: Torit and Kapoeta East (Eastern Equatoria State)
Gogrial West and Tonj South (Warrap State)
Three additional counties to be selected based on Project Targeting Index
geographic_scope.fragile_or_conflict_affected_areas: yes - extensive. South Sudan is highly fragile with history of recurrent conflict and violence. Project operates in conflict-affected areas with subnational violence and insecurity persisting. At least 3,414 civilians were victims of violent incidents in 2021. Project includes two refugee-hosting counties with 333,673 refugees (93% from Sudan). Areas are remote, isolated, exposed to natural disasters, and have limited infrastructure.

key_components.component_name: Cash Transfer and Complementary Social Measures
key_components.description: Provides cash assistance to poor and vulnerable households through two modalities: (1) Cash for Labor-Intensive Public Works (LIPW) - 67,200 HHs receive 18 months of cash at US$2.7/day for 15 days/month in exchange for participation in public works or behavioral change training; (2) Direct Income Support (DIS) - 28,800 labor-constrained HHs receive unconditional cash transfers. Includes complementary social measures (WASH, nutrition, ECD training) for select beneficiaries. Covers refugees and host communities.

Supports 4,500 households (subset of cash transfer beneficiaries) with economic opportunities targeting youth aged 18-35 (85% of beneficiaries, 50% female). Includes business/livelihoods training, coaching/mentorship, livelihood grants (US$250 in two installments), savings groups formation, and financial services access. Implemented in 3 locations (Juba confirmed, 2 others TBD). Includes impact evaluation in Juba and mobile money payment pilot.

key_components.component_name: Strengthened Institutional Capacity and Social Protection System
key_components.description: Builds capacity of MGCSW to lead and coordinate social protection sector and eventually establish government-led national safety net program. Activities include: institutional capacity enhancement of MGCSW SP Unit; strengthening of MIS, biometric systems, and GRM; support for SP sector development including NSPPF review and implementation framework; support for NSPWG and MIS Technical Working Group; capacity building for CRA.

key_components.component_name: Project Management, Monitoring and Evaluation, and Knowledge Generation
key_components.description: Supports day-to-day project management by MAFS PCU; comprehensive M&E framework using electronic data collection in MIS; third-party monitoring; process evaluation; impact assessments; learning agenda documentation; capacity building for CRA; knowledge generation on social and economic interventions.

implementation_arrangements.implementing_agency: Ministry of Agriculture and Food Security (MAFS) as lead implementing agency with Ministry of Gender, Child and Social Welfare (MGCSW) implementing Component 3 and participating in monitoring. UNOPS contracted through Standard Output Agreement for cash transfer implementation (sub-components 1.1 and 1.2). Specialized NGOs/firms hired for complementary social measures and economic opportunities. Project Coordination Units (PCUs) established in both MAFS and MGCSW.
implementation_arrangements.coordination_mechanisms: National Advisory Committee (NAC) chaired by MAFS, co-chaired by Ministry of Finance and Planning and MGCSW, including CRA and UNHCR for policy/strategic guidance. National Technical Committee (NTC) for technical/operational support. Local institutional and community governance structures at state and county levels. Coordination with UNHCR and CRA for refugee-hosting areas. Synergies with other World Bank projects (ECRP-I/II, RALP, ELRP, PEHSP, WSEEP, COVID-19 health response).
implementation_arrangements.procurement_approach: World Bank Procurement Regulations (July 1, 2016, revised November 2017 and August 2020). Project Procurement Strategy for Development (PPSD) prepared. Procurement methods include international competitive bidding, national competitive bidding, procurement from UN agencies, shopping, single source selection, direct contracting. Procurement from UN agencies for high-risk activities. Prior and post-review thresholds applied. Procurement plan updated at least biannually. Advance contracting initiated before effectiveness.
implementation_arrangements.monitoring_and_evaluation: Comprehensive M&E framework using electronic data collection in MIS with real-time data analysis. Registration Lessons Learned surveys, Post Distribution Monitoring, routine monitoring of LIPW and complementary measures, citizen engagement indicators. Third-Party Monitoring (TPM) agency contracted for quarterly monitoring reports and independent verification. Joint supervision by MAFS and MGCSW. Process evaluation and impact evaluation (Component 2 in Juba). M&E functions integrated into project MIS. Quarterly interim financial reports. Annual external audit by National Audit Chamber. Internal audit by Internal Audit Directorate of Ministry of Finance and Planning.

identified_risks.risk_description: While 2018 peace agreement largely holding, political landscape has risks of shifting power dynamics and resurgence of national-level political fragility and violence. Elite capture at various levels, discrimination based on ethnicity/gender/religion, GBV concerns, and fiduciary mismanagement risks. Low fiscal commitment to social sectors poses risk to sustainability.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Security Management Plan. Risk assessment in all project locations to identify risks and fault lines. Robust GRM. Community sensitization and awareness raising. Transparent and participatory processes. Biometric systems to prevent fraud. Third-party monitoring. Regular supervision missions.

identified_risks.risk_description: High inflation (43% in FY2020/21), currency volatility, declining oil production, high debt distress risk (59.5% of GDP), government salary arrears (5 months), limited access to concessional financing, oil price volatility, weak budget processes, debt transparency issues. Recent shocks (COVID-19, floods, locust) threaten economic gains.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: IMF Staff Monitored Program with focus on fiscal discipline, monetary/exchange rate reform, debt management, governance strengthening. Recent IMF allocation of SDR 235.8 million. World Bank engagement on PFM reforms. Cash transfer amounts denominated in US Dollar to mitigate foreign exchange fluctuations. Direct disbursements to UNOPS to reduce foreign exchange exposure.

identified_risks.risk_description: Safety net sector lacks overarching strategic and policy guidance despite NSPPF launch. Sector remains primarily financed/implemented by external partners resulting in fragmented programs. Limited government leadership and ownership. Weak coordination despite efforts to address gaps.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Project used as platform to strengthen government capacity and leadership in SP sector. Revitalize coordination platform to operationalize sector policy. World Bank leverages convening role to bring together development and humanitarian partners. Support for NSPPF review and implementation framework development. Support for NSPWG and MIS Technical Working Group functioning.

identified_risks.risk_description: Cash provision in South Sudan inherently high risk requiring physical cash transfer. Influx of cash could place beneficiaries at risk of violence, GBV, and elite capture. Limited evidence on complementary social measures and economic opportunities in FCV contexts. Complexity of targeting and registration in insecure areas.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Build on SSSNP experience. Cash distributed in secure community locations with minimal advance notice. Mobile money payment pilot in Juba. Continuous risk monitoring and assessment. Sensitization activities. Rigorous GRM for exclusion/error/fraud/corruption concerns. Third-party monitoring. Biometric systems for accurate beneficiary identification. Flexible implementation approach to waive labor requirements during crises.

identified_risks.risk_category: Institutional Capacity for Implementation and Sustainability
identified_risks.risk_description: Government faces significant capacity and resource constraints. MAFS has experience but limited capacity; MGCSW lacks capacity and previous Bank project experience. Implementation in certain geographic locations poses significant risk due to high security risks and very limited infrastructure. Low capacity of local-level structures and community members. Safety of project staff and committee members in highly insecure areas. Weak institutional capacity at national and state/county levels.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: PCU capacity enhanced through deployment of key personnel with requisite experience. Technical assistance and capacity building support on key aspects. In-depth community mobilization and sensitization through robust communication campaigns. Robust GRM to address grievances timely and transparently. UNOPS contracted for implementation due to proven capacity and local presence. Capacity building plan for MGCSW. Support for on-boarding of key management and technical staff. Training on procurement, financial management, M&E. Hands-on enhanced implementation support from World Bank.

identified_risks.risk_description: Substantial overall rating. Social risks include: social exclusion and elite capture, insufficient community engagement, lack of participation of women/youth/vulnerable groups, use of child labor, sexual harassment and sexual exploitation and abuse (SH/SEA). Environmental risks from urban works include: solid waste generation, soil erosion, noise/vibrations, dust emissions, water/soil pollution, air pollution, loss of vegetation, occupational health/safety hazards, COVID-19 spread risks. Potential for GBV risks from cash transfers.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: Environmental and Social Commitment Plan (ESCP) and stakeholder engagement plan prepared and disclosed. ESMF being updated for SNSOP with lessons from SSSNP. Framework approach for managing E&S risks. Negative list of excluded activities. Screening of subprojects. Labor Management Procedures. Comprehensive Social Assessment. Security risk assessment and management plan. SH/SEA prevention and response action plan. Sub-Saharan African historically underserved traditional local communities assessment and plan. Gender-sensitive GRM with multiple reporting channels and separate processing for GBV. Code of conduct for all participants. GBV Action Plan. Community-based targeting with emphasis on inclusion. Third-party monitoring. Regular World Bank implementation support missions. Capacity building trainings for implementing agencies.

identified_risks.risk_description: High rating. Nonfunctional public procurement legal/regulative framework, weak institutional operational capacity, intercommunity conflicts in project areas. South Sudan ranked most corrupt in 2021 Corruption Perception Index. Risks of double registration of beneficiaries leading to double payments, validation of beneficiaries at payment point, confirmation of receipt by intended beneficiaries. Difficulty ensuring effective monitoring/supervision of decentralized activities. Access challenges due to conflict, natural disasters, travel/communication constraints.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: UNOPS contracted as implementing agency with requisite capacity and local presence. Biometric system for safe/accurate beneficiary registration avoiding double registration. Biometric validation at payment point. Actual cash transfers contracted to Financial Service Provider (FSP) transferring risks to FSP. Mobile money payment pilot in Juba to mitigate security/operational risks. Third-party Monitoring for regular monitoring of cash transfers and quarterly reporting. Internal audit by Internal Audit Directorate of Ministry of Finance and Planning. External audit by National Audit Chamber. Capacity support and technical assistance to audit functions. Direct payments to UNOPS from Grant Account. Designated Account (DA) in US Dollar. Local currency accounts kept at minimum. Qualified FM personnel at MAFS and MGCSW. Senior Financial Management Specialist in PCU. Quarterly interim financial reports. Project Operation Manual with detailed procedures. Advance contracting of UNOPS before effectiveness.

identified_risks.risk_description: Moderate rating. While South Sudan has progressive refugee legal framework and government commitment, implementation hindered by capacity limitations of CRA and other government institutions, and challenging socioeconomic environment. Risk of inadequate refugee protection framework adequacy.
identified_risks.mitigation_measures: World Bank confirmed adequacy of South Sudan's refugee protection framework in consultation with UNHCR (assessment dated February 20, 2022). South Sudan has one of East Africa's more progressive refugee frameworks granting refugees rights to move, settle, work, and benefit from public services. Little risk of backsliding on government's strong policy and demonstrated commitment. Focused capacity building efforts through this and other Bank projects will increase government's ability to implement progressive refugee framework. Close engagement with UNHCR and CRA on WHR-supported activities. Equitable targeting of refugees and host communities. Community-based targeting mechanism adapted for refugee/host areas. Coordination with UNHCR for access and facilitation.

triggered_safeguard_policies: Assessment and Management of Environmental and Social Risks and Impacts (ESS1)
Resource Efficiency and Pollution Prevention and Management (ESS3)
Land Acquisition, Restrictions on Land Use and Involuntary Resettlement (ESS5)
Biodiversity Conservation and Sustainable Management of Living Natural Resources (ESS6)
Indigenous Peoples/Sub-Saharan African Historically Underserved Traditional Local Communities (ESS7)
safeguards_and_social.grievance_redress_mechanism: yes - Comprehensive GRM established. Community-based GRM with multiple channels for reporting grievances. Gender- and GBV-sensitive GRM with separate processing system for GBV-related grievances. Survivor-centric approach prioritizing safety and consent. Anonymous reporting options. Monthly GRM reports reviewed. Target of 85% grievance resolution rate. GRM tied to M&E function with regular reporting. Accessible and responsive GRM strengthening citizen engagement. Code of conduct for all project participants with clear processes for breaches.
safeguards_and_social.gender_considerations: Strong gender focus throughout project. At least 65% of LIPW direct cash recipients will be women. 50% of youth receiving economic opportunities will be female. Female-headed households prioritized for DIS. At least 45% female participation in community structures. Gender-disaggregated data in all M&E activities. Gender-sensitive LIPW design including: women's priorities in sub-project selection, sub-projects contributing to nutrition/ECD, childcare at LIPW sites, 3-hour workdays for women vs 4-hour for men, less intensive tasks for women. Complementary social measures targeting mothers and female caregivers on WASH, nutrition, ECD. Behavioral change communication on gender issues and GBV targeting female beneficiaries, male partners, fathers, community leaders. Male champions engaged for gender-transformative interventions. GBV Action Plan developed. Gender-sensitive GRM. Awareness raising on gender issues during project activities. Support for female youth economic empowerment.
vulnerable_groups: Chronically ill and people with disabilities

fcv_relevant_details.conflict_sensitivity: Project explicitly designed with conflict sensitivity. Addresses FCV drivers through: (1) Providing immediate support to most vulnerable affected by conflict-induced poverty and displacement; (2) Building resilience and human capital to support recovery; (3) Creating livelihood opportunities to address youth idleness and reduce turning to crime/violence; (4) Strengthening social cohesion through public works bringing diverse ethnic groups together for community benefit; (5) Supporting refugees and host communities to mitigate tensions from competition for services/livelihoods/resources; (6) Leveraging refugee-host community committees for coordination and social cohesion; (7) Strengthening humanitarian-development-peace nexus; (8) Addressing GBV and harmful social norms through behavioral change communication; (9) Flexible implementation to respond to conflict/security changes; (10) Security Management Plan and risk assessments in all locations; (11) Nuanced communication promoting peaceful cooperation and unity among diverse groups.
fcv_relevant_details.fragility_context: South Sudan is highly fragile with decades of armed conflict before and after 2011 independence. Nearly 5 years of conflict (2013-2018) formally ended with Revitalized Agreement in September 2018, but subnational violence and insecurity persist. In 2021, at least 3,414 civilians were victims of violent incidents. Conflict-induced poverty, displacement, and trauma weakened kinship and community ties. Economy contracted 5.1% in FY2020/21 with poverty increasing to 79.3%. Between April-July 2022, 7.74 million people (two-thirds of population) faced acute food insecurity with 87,000 in famine conditions. 2 million people including 1.3 million children under 5 expected acutely malnourished in 2022. Recurring climate shocks (flooding, drought, locust) compound vulnerabilities. Government spending on social services is low with weak institutional capacity. South Sudan ranked 172 of 174 on Human Capital Index (0.31). Hosts 333,673 refugees (93% from Sudan) in remote, isolated areas. Weak rule of law, high crime, limited economic opportunities especially for youth. Restrictive gender norms and high GBV prevalence (65% of women experienced physical/sexual violence in conflict-affected locations).
fcv_relevant_details.security_considerations: High security risks throughout project areas. Subnational violence and insecurity persist in large parts of country. Project operates in conflict-affected former opposition areas. Safety of project staff and committee members is concern, particularly risk of violent attacks in highly insecure areas. Limited infrastructure and access constraints due to conflict. Sporadic localized violence and recurring weather shocks create volatile context. Project includes Security Management Plan and risk assessments in all locations. Flexible implementation to waive labor requirements during insecurity. Cash distributed in secure community locations with minimal advance notice to minimize mobilization by armed groups. Continuous risk monitoring and assessment. Sensitization to preempt/mitigate risks. UNOPS has proven ability to negotiate access and implement in most remote and conflict-affected areas.
fcv_relevant_details.displacement_or_refugees: yes - significant. South Sudan hosts 333,673 refugees (93% from Sudan) under open-door policy. Vast majority (95%) hosted in two areas: Jamjang in Ruweng Administrative Area and Maban County in Upper Nile State (far north). Also hosted in far south and far east regions. Areas are highly remote, isolated, exposed to natural disasters (floods). Refugees and host communities largely dependent on humanitarian assistance with limited livelihood opportunities. Socioeconomic conditions and vulnerability remain dire. Project targets 7,000 refugee households and 10,500 host community households in two pre-selected refugee-hosting counties (Maban and Pariang). Same cash transfer amounts, frequency, duration as other beneficiaries (18 months at US$2.7/day for 15 days/month). Refugees and host communities included in LIPW and complementary social measures based on feasibility assessment. Community-based HH targeting mechanism adapted for refugee/host areas. Strong coordination with UNHCR for access and facilitation. Project supports Government's Community Empowerment and Socioeconomic Development Strategy for Refugee Hosting Areas. Promotes local integration of refugees with host communities as durable solution. Transitions from humanitarian dependence to development-focused approach. Mitigates latent/potential conflicts from competition for services/livelihoods/resources. Leverages established refugee-host community committees for coordination and social cohesion. Equitable targeting to avoid feelings of exclusion among hosts. Commission for Refugee Affairs (CRA) capacity building supported. World Bank confirmed adequacy of South Sudan's refugee protection framework in consultation with UNHCR.
